context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i'm stuck on PS3 prob 1, esp part (c) w the derived variable. i kinda get the state machine parts but idk how the base-\(n\) hint is supposed to help.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we dig into the hint, let me ask: if you treat the list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}) as the digits of a base-n number — so a0 is the most significant digit — what numerical value does that give you? Try a small example, like n=3 with the permutation (2, 0, 1).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about part (c) and the base-n hint. I should use the Socratic method to guide them without giving the answer. A good first step is to check what they already understand about the hint before diving in, and to ask a question that moves them toward the insight that treating the permutation as a base-n number gives a numerical value that can serve as the derived variable. I'll ask a targeted question to get them thinking about what treating the list as a base-n number actually means numerically.

exchanges.student: i think it'd be \(2\cdot 3^2+0\cdot 3+1=19\). so in general maybe \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a_k n^{\,n-1-k}\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! Now here's the key question: when you make a good reversal on an inverted pair (i, j) — remember, that means a_i > a_j — what happens to that base-n number? Does it go up or down?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed the base-n value and generalized it. This is great progress. Now I should push them toward the key insight: how does this value change when a good reversal is made? Specifically, does it increase or decrease? I'll ask them to think about what a good reversal does to this numerical value, which is the crux of part (c).

exchanges.student: i'm guessing down, but i'm not fully seeing why the middle reversed part doesn't maybe mess it up. is the idea that the first changed digit becomes \(a_j\), which is smaller than \(a_i\), so that dominates?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! Think about it this way: what is the maximum possible increase the middle digits could contribute? And how does that compare to the decrease at position i? Try bounding both quantities in terms of n.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition and is close to the key insight. They correctly identified that the most significant changed digit (position i) decreases from a_i to a_j, and are wondering whether the middle part could offset this. This is exactly the right thing to think about. I should guide them to think more carefully about the magnitude of the change at position i versus the maximum possible change from all the middle digits combined, without giving the answer directly.

exchanges.student: ok wait, since digits are \(0\) to \(n-1\), changing later positions can only affect smaller powers of \(n\). but idk how to write the bound cleanly rn—do i compare the drop at place \(i\) to a geometric sum for places \(i+1\) to \(j\)?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, exactly that comparison. So try it: what's the minimum drop at position i (in terms of n^{n-1-i}), and what's the maximum total change from positions i+1 through j? Write out the geometric sum and see what you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea and is very close to formalizing the proof. They correctly identified comparing the drop at position i to a geometric sum for positions i+1 to j. I should affirm this direction and nudge them to actually compute/bound both quantities rather than doing it for them.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think the drop at position \(i\) is at least \(1\cdot n^{\,n-1-i}\) since \(a_i-a_j\ge 1\). then the later positions can change by at most \((n-1)\sum_{k=i+1}^{j} n^{\,n-1-k}\), but i'm not sure if that comes out strictly smaller.
exchanges.tutor: Good setup! Now just compute that geometric sum — what does $(n-1)\sum_{k=i+1}^{j} n^{n-1-k}$ simplify to? (It's a finite geometric series.)
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has set up the comparison correctly. They need to evaluate the geometric sum and compare it to n^{n-1-i}. This is a straightforward geometric series computation that they should be able to do themselves. I'll prompt them to just compute that geometric sum.

exchanges.student: i get \((n-1)\sum_{k=i+1}^{j} n^{n-1-k}=(n-1)\sum_{t=0}^{j-i-1} n^t = n^{\,j-i}-1\), right? then i'm stuck bc the drop term has an extra factor \(n^{\,n-1-i}\), so i feel like i changed indices weirdly.
exchanges.tutor: Check your substitution carefully — when k goes from i+1 to j, the exponent n-1-k doesn't start at 0. What is n-1-k when k=i+1? Redo the substitution keeping track of that starting value.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made an index substitution error. They correctly computed (n-1) times the geometric sum as n^{j-i}-1, but they dropped the n^{n-1-i} factor that should be there. The sum should be (n-1) * n^{n-1-j} * (n^{j-i} - 1)/(n-1) or similar. I should point out the error and ask them to redo the substitution carefully without doing it for them.

exchanges.student: ohhh right, when \(k=i+1\) the exponent is \(n-2-i\), not 0. so maybe it should be \((n-1)\sum_{r=0}^{j-i-1} n^{\,n-2-i-r}\), and that equals \(n^{\,n-1-i}-n^{\,n-1-j}\), which is \(< n^{\,n-1-i}\)?
exchanges.tutor: That's it! So the net change is at most -(n^{n-1-i}) + (n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}) = -n^{n-1-j} < 0. You've got the core argument. Now, can you also verify property (i) — why does your derived variable only take nonneg integer values, and is it bounded below?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed the geometric sum and arrived at the key inequality. The maximum possible increase from middle digits is n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}, which is strictly less than n^{n-1-i}, the minimum drop at position i. This means the net change is strictly negative, proving the derived variable strictly decreases. I should affirm this and ask them to now state the full proof clearly, including both properties (i) and (ii) required by the problem.

exchanges.student: i think yes: it's a sum of digits times powers of \(n\), so it's an integer, and all digits are nonneg so the value is \(\ge 0\). bounded below by 0, so w the strict decrease that should give termination, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. You now have all the ingredients. Try writing up a complete proof for part (c) on your own — state the derived variable, prove property (i), then prove property (ii) using the argument we just worked through.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both properties and connected them to termination. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate everything into a written proof attempt, since we've now covered all the key ideas across several messages. This satisfies the rule about asking for written integration after ~3 messages on the same idea.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, i think i can do that now. can u just sanity check one thing: for part (d), i should say nonnegative integers can't decrease forever, so every execution terminates, and by part (b) any terminal state is sorted?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the right structure. Make sure your writeup explicitly names the principle that guarantees a strictly decreasing sequence of nonneg integers must terminate. Do you know what that principle is called?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea for part (d) and is essentially summarizing the well-ordering principle argument correctly. This is good and I can affirm it briefly without giving more than necessary, since they clearly understand it.

exchanges.student: is that the well-ordering principle / descent principle? i always mix those up lol.
exchanges.tutor: They're closely related! The well-ordering principle says every nonempty set of nonneg integers has a minimum. Infinite descent says you can't have an infinite strictly decreasing sequence of nonneg integers. Can you see why those two statements are essentially equivalent, and which one maps more naturally onto your termination argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about the distinction between well-ordering principle and infinite descent. Both are closely related and either could work here. I should briefly clarify the distinction and let the student decide which framing fits their writeup, without writing it for them.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session on Problem 1(c)/(d). Every key reasoning step was elicited from the student through targeted questions rather than being given outright. The tutor built progressively on correct student contributions, corrected a specific index error with a diagnostic prompt rather than just supplying the fix, and kept responses concise and focused throughout. Tone was consistently supportive and formative.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the derived variable, the bound comparison, or the termination argument directly; each was drawn out of the student via questions. No near-submission-ready output was produced. 1.2: The tutor acknowledged every correct student step and diagnosed the index-substitution error in Turn 6 with a targeted question before explaining anything. 1.3: The tutor gave explicit process-level guidance (e.g., 'write out the geometric sum', 'state the derived variable, prove property (i), then property (ii)') throughout. 2.1/2.2: The conversation progressed without repetition or drift. 3.1/3.2: Responses were short, precise, and consistently encouraging without implying formal grading.
